subject: Skill Education
topicTitle: Service Orientation: User at the Centre
sourceLabel: Based on NCERT Kaushal Vikas (Grade 9) + AI-generated teacher-style explanation

studyContent.title: Service Orientation: User at the Centre
studyContent.intro: Keeping the person receiving the service at the centre of every decision.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Service orientation is the way of thinking that values people's needs, acts with care and finds satisfaction in serving others; if a salesperson listens patiently and guides without irritation, the user feels at ease and is likely to return. Service orientation comprises four key qualities: keeping the user at the centre, fair and ethical decision-making, contribution to society, and organisation and teamwork. Being centred on the user means that every service exists to meet someone's need, so before starting one must ask who is being served and what they really need, and think about how users can receive the service as intended. This is illustrated by a flood relief camp where, despite makeshift space, planning was user-centred with portable toilets, a first-aid centre, and organised sections for grocery, clothing, bedding and toys, while ensuring displaced and distressed people are treated with utmost respect and sensitivity.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Service orientation means always thinking about the person you are serving. Before doing anything, ask who needs help and what they really want, and arrange things so they can use the service easily.
stepByStep: Adopt the mindset: Value people's needs and find satisfaction in serving.
Ask who you serve: Identify the user and their real needs.
Plan for the user: Arrange the service so users receive it as intended.
Cater to all needs: Consider the elderly, children and persons with disabilities.
Treat with respect: Maintain dignity and sensitivity, especially for the distressed.

keywords.example: Sensitivity helps comfort distressed people.

studyContent.title: Fair and Ethical Decision-making
studyContent.intro: Making decisions that are fair, safe, trustworthy and inclusive for all.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Fair and ethical decision-making is about reflecting on a situation from all angles and choosing what is correct; when users know they will be treated fairly they feel respected and service quality improves. In practice this means choosing actions that are fair, safe, trustworthy and inclusive, including following agreed processes like maintaining a queue while making exceptions such as earmarked seats for the elderly, treating everyone with dignity and maintaining privacy like closing the door while treating a patient, making decisions that consider everyone's needs rather than only the loudest, and maintaining confidentiality by not sharing data without permission, as a photographer cannot share images without consent. Because unexpected situations are hard to judge, rules and protocols are established so all service providers know what to do, which also helps manage conflict, as in deciding fairly how to handle a person who cannot read the queue signs.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Being fair means treating everyone correctly and equally, keeping a proper queue, protecting people's privacy, and not sharing their information without permission. Clear rules help everyone know the right thing to do.
stepByStep: Reflect on the situation: Consider all angles before deciding.
Follow agreed processes: Maintain queues while allowing fair exceptions.
Protect dignity and privacy: Treat all with respect and keep matters private.
Consider everyone's needs: Serve fairly, not just the loudest.
Maintain confidentiality: Do not share data without permission.

keywords.example: Confidentiality protects patient data.

keywords.example: A protocol guides queue management.

studyContent.title: Contribution to Society and Teamwork
studyContent.intro: How service contributes to society and depends on organisation and teamwork.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Every act of service, big or small, helps improve society, and when we understand others' needs and respond with patience, service does not feel like a burden; simple actions like helping a neighbour read a message or keeping a shared space clean build a kinder community, and in professional spaces these become professional services. During the COVID-19 pandemic, doctors, nurses, sanitation workers, delivery staff, police and volunteers continued to serve, showing that service through care, communication, safety and support is the backbone of every community. Organisation and teamwork are also essential because many services depend on a chain of service where one person's work affects the next, as a waiter must communicate with kitchen staff and nurses changing shifts must inform the next person of the patient's needs. Organisation involves detailed planning, ensuring tools and materials, scheduling work, assigning responsibility and having standard processes so the same quality is provided each time.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Helping others, even in small ways, makes society better. Many services work like a chain, so people must work as a team and plan well, like a waiter telling the kitchen the order, so everyone reaches the same goal.
stepByStep: Value every act: See small acts of service as improving society.
Respond with patience: Understand needs so service is not a burden.
Recall the pandemic: Note how professionals kept serving in COVID-19.
Work as a chain: Communicate so one person's work supports the next.
Organise the work: Plan, schedule and assign responsibility for quality.

keywords.example: Teamwork runs a health camp smoothly.

keywords.example: Organisation assigns each member a role.

studyContent.title: Creating Service Environments
studyContent.intro: Building a clean physical space, warm social setting and safe conditions.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Service environment comprises both the physical and social environment, including safety. The physical environment should be clean, well-organised and safe, raising questions such as whether the place is clean with well-placed furniture, well-ventilated without bad odour, and equipped with basic amenities like clean bathrooms, a water dispenser and a first-aid kit. The social environment is created by interaction with the service user and the provider's attitude, attire and communication; even a well-furnished dental clinic loses users if the assistant is cold or cancels appointments without notice. Safety in services is not just about avoiding accidents but about building trust, so users feel their needs will be met, they will be heard and respected, and their information kept confidential, following the Three Cs of cleanliness, care and caution. A good service environment is also about how smoothly things work, with everyone knowing what to do and having a backup plan.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A good service place is clean, comfortable and safe. It is not only about how it looks but also how people are treated, with politeness and care, and how smoothly everything runs.
stepByStep: Arrange the physical space: Keep it clean, ventilated and well-furnished with amenities.
Create the social setting: Ensure polite attitude, attire and communication.
Build trust through safety: Make users feel heard, respected and confidential.
Follow the Three Cs: Apply cleanliness, care and caution.
Ensure smooth working: Have clear roles and a backup plan.

studyContent.title: Consent in Human Services
studyContent.intro: Using a consent form to agree on the service and build trust.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: In human services a consent form is an agreement between the service provider and the person being helped, telling the person exactly the service they will receive and what is expected of them in return. Consent acknowledges the right of the service user to make decisions about their comfort and safety and helps build a safe, trusting relationship. It is rooted in respect for every person's right to control what happens to their body and life, meaning service users must have a complete understanding of what is offered and willingly give permission before any action, and be aware of potential risks however minor. Consent is critical when data is involved, as the provider must ensure the user understands what will be done with any data they provide, and importantly consent can be withdrawn at any point.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A consent form is an agreement that clearly tells a person what service they will get and asks their permission first. It builds trust, and the person can say no or change their mind at any time.
stepByStep: Explain the service: Tell the person exactly what they will receive.
State expectations: Make clear what is expected from each side.
Seek willing permission: Ensure the user understands and agrees freely.
Inform of risks: Make the user aware of any potential risks.
Allow withdrawal: Let consent be withdrawn at any point.

keywords.example: Consent can be withdrawn at any time.

studyContent.title: Service Quality Criteria
studyContent.intro: Quality parameters before, during and after a service.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Working in services involves step-by-step practices followed before, during and after the service, categorised into input, process and output stages, each with quality parameters. At the input stage, before starting, the needs of users are identified, the space is clean and hygienic, tools and materials are procured, a task checklist is prepared, consent is received, and the environment is oriented towards efficient processes. At the process stage, while delivering, there is smooth workflow and coordination, timely and respectful interaction, adherence to rules and protocols, safe and hygienic practices including cyber safety where relevant, active listening and problem-solving, and confidentiality where relevant. At the output stage, after the service, there is cleaning and safe waste disposal, feedback collected on user satisfaction and comfort, and follow-up on feedback for improvement.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Good service follows quality steps at three stages: before, you prepare cleanly and take consent; during, you work smoothly, politely and safely; after, you clean up and collect feedback to improve.
stepByStep: Prepare the input: Identify needs, clean the space and take consent.
Procure resources: Gather tools, materials and a task checklist.
Deliver the process: Work smoothly, politely, safely and confidentially.
Listen and solve: Use active listening and problem-solving.
Complete the output: Clean up, collect feedback and follow up.

keywords.meaning: The delivery during a service.
keywords.example: Polite interaction is a process-stage parameter.

keywords.example: Collecting feedback is an output-stage step.

studyContent.title: Technology and AI in Services
studyContent.intro: Using technology and AI responsibly to support service delivery.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Service providers can use AI and technology in various ways to support delivery. Technology can help by providing digital maps, signs and kiosks, while AI-powered chat assistants can be trained to answer common questions and multi-language translations and audio guides can be offered through AI tools. AI can also handle tasks that usually consume human time, such as auto-filling forms, collecting and analysing feedback, and sending automatic reminders for safety checks or materials. At the same time, safety and ethical use must always be kept in mind. This technology supplements human service by freeing providers to focus on care and interaction, while ensuring that automation is used responsibly and that users' data and trust are protected.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Technology and AI can make services easier, with digital maps, chat assistants and auto-filled forms. But they must be used safely and ethically, always protecting people's information and trust.
stepByStep: Provide digital tools: Offer maps, signs and kiosks to guide users.
Use AI assistants: Train chat assistants to answer common questions.
Offer translations: Provide multi-language and audio guides through AI.
Automate routine tasks: Auto-fill forms and send safety reminders.
Use it ethically: Keep safety and protect users' data and trust.

studyContent.title: Introduction to Healthcare
studyContent.intro: How healthcare brings preventive and curative care close to people.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Health is the foundation of life, since healthy people can learn, work and enjoy daily activities. Looking after health is not limited to visiting a hospital when ill; it is shaped daily by clean surroundings, safe water, nutritious food, physical activity, mental well-being, timely care and support from others. Healthcare focuses on these everyday needs and brings health services closer to where people live, and India has a long tradition of both preventive and curative practices. In rural areas, Auxiliary Nurse Midwives (ANMs) focus on maternal and child health and immunisation, while Accredited Social Health Activist (ASHA) workers are community volunteers who spread awareness about nutrition, hygiene and family planning and provide basic first aid; both were recognised as Corona Warriors during COVID-19. The ancient Charaka-Samhita described principles of healthy living, disease prevention, diet and treatment, many of which still influence modern wellness in India.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Health is the base of a good life. Healthcare is not just about hospitals; it is about clean water, good food, exercise and timely care. Workers like ASHA and ANM bring this care close to villages.
stepByStep: See health's importance: Recognise that healthy people can learn and work.
Look beyond hospitals: Note that daily habits shape health too.
Bring care close: Understand how healthcare reaches where people live.
Know the workers: Recall ANMs and ASHA workers in rural areas.
Value tradition: Note the Charaka-Samhita's lasting principles.

studyContent.title: Scoping and Process Chart for Healthcare
studyContent.intro: Deciding the health service to provide and listing the tasks.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Scoping the healthcare work means deciding the type of service, such as organising a health awareness camp, taking part in hygiene and sanitation activities, or creating materials about nutrition, fitness and basic first aid, while remembering that the service should focus on support, awareness and assistance and must not provide medical diagnoses or treatments. Decisions also cover availability of resources and support such as a nurse, doctor or local health worker, the usefulness of the service to the school or community, and where it will be provided considering safety, cleanliness, space and ease of setup. A process chart then lists the tasks with dates and responsible people, including identifying experts, learning to use instruments for health parameters, collecting home remedies, identifying occupational health risks, setting up the health camp, delivering services, waste disposal, and feedback collection and reflection.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Decide what health service you can offer, like an awareness camp, remembering you cannot diagnose or treat illness. Then list every task, from learning to use instruments to setting up the camp and collecting feedback.
stepByStep: Choose the service: Pick an awareness camp, hygiene activity or first-aid material.
Stay within limits: Provide support and awareness, not diagnosis or treatment.
Check resources: Identify needed support like a nurse or doctor.
Choose the location: Consider safety, cleanliness and space.
Make the process chart: List tasks with dates and responsibility.

topicTitle: Selecting Materials and Tools for Healthcare

studyContent.title: Selecting Materials and Tools for Healthcare
studyContent.intro: Gathering hygiene materials and basic health-monitoring instruments safely.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Once the tasks are clear, materials and tools are listed and collected. Materials include personal protective equipment like gloves and masks to maintain hygiene, using a new pair for each person and disposing of used items in a covered biomedical waste bin; a waste bin with separate sections for regular and biomedical waste; a measuring tape and weighing scale for height and body parameters; and yoga mats kept clean and dry on a flat surface. Tools generally used in healthcare include a thermometer to measure body temperature, sanitised before and after each use; a pulse oximeter or smartwatch to measure oxygen saturation and pulse, with a clean dry finger; a stopwatch for pulse, breathing rate or exercise duration; a blood pressure monitor whose cuff is wrapped properly and not overinflated; and a glucometer used only for observation. Safety notes accompany each tool, and all health-related tasks must be done only under adult supervision.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Gather hygiene items like gloves and masks, and basic tools like a thermometer and oximeter. Use a new pair of gloves for each person, sanitise tools, and always work under an adult's supervision.
stepByStep: Gather PPE: Collect gloves and masks, a new pair per person.
Set up waste bins: Use separate bins for regular and biomedical waste.
Collect measuring items: Get a measuring tape and weighing scale.
Select health tools: Use a thermometer, oximeter and BP monitor safely.
Sanitise and supervise: Clean tools and work under adult supervision.

studyContent.title: Bill of Materials and Consent
studyContent.intro: Estimating costs of the health service and taking informed consent.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: A Bill of Materials helps estimate costs in advance and avoid waste by ensuring only what is necessary is bought, and it includes both material costs and labour costs. For a health camp it might list disposable gloves, hand sanitiser, face masks, a borrowed first-aid kit, stationery for posters, and borrowed BP monitor, oximeter and thermometer, plus labour for cleaning, preparing awareness material, registration and setting up the camp. A consent form, as learnt in Chapter 9, is a simple way to communicate that both the student team and the person receiving the service clearly understand what is offered; it is not a legal document but builds trust, avoids confusion and ensures safety. When taking consent the team shares the healthcare services to be provided, shares safety expectations, clarifies that it is not a substitute for medical treatment, and takes consent for health data and photographs.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Make a cost list of items like gloves and masks plus the labour involved. Then use a consent form to clearly tell visitors what you will do, including that you cannot replace a doctor, and get their agreement.
stepByStep: List materials: Note gloves, sanitiser, masks and borrowed instruments.
Add labour costs: Include time for cleaning, setup and registration.
Prepare the consent form: Communicate the services clearly to users.
Clarify the limits: State that it is not a substitute for medical treatment.
Take data consent: Seek permission for health data and photographs.

keywords.example: The form shares safety expectations.

keywords.example: Informed consent covers health data use.

studyContent.title: Monitoring Health Parameters
studyContent.intro: Safely measuring vital signs like temperature, blood pressure and oxygen.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Vital signs are important body signals that help understand how well the body is functioning, and learners practise measuring peers' vitals under supervision. Instruments and their normal values include the thermometer measuring body temperature, with 97-99 degree F normal and above 99 degree F a fever; the blood pressure monitor measuring the force blood uses through arteries, with less than 120/80 mmHg normal and above 140/90 needing medical attention; the pulse oximeter measuring oxygen saturation, with 98-100 per cent normal and below 94 needing urgent attention; and the glucometer for blood sugar, used for demonstration only. Important reminders are that these tasks must only be done under adult supervision, learners should not diagnose or interpret results but only practise safe measurement and recording, and if any reading seems abnormal a teacher or practitioner must be called immediately.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Vital signs like temperature and blood pressure tell us how the body is doing. You can practise measuring them with tools like a thermometer and oximeter, but only under an adult's watch, and never try to diagnose.
stepByStep: Understand vital signs: See them as signals of how the body functions.
Use the thermometer: Measure temperature, with 97-99 degree F normal.
Use the BP monitor: Measure blood pressure, with under 120/80 normal.
Use the oximeter: Measure oxygen, with 98-100 per cent normal.
Record, do not diagnose: Only measure and record, calling an adult if abnormal.

keywords.meaning: The force of blood moving through arteries.
keywords.example: Normal BP is under 120/80 mmHg.

keywords.meaning: The percentage of oxygen in the blood.

studyContent.title: Home Remedies for Common Ailments
studyContent.intro: Preparing safe traditional remedies for minor everyday complaints.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Traditional home remedies are often used for minor everyday health complaints, made using natural ingredients such as herbs, spices and household items; they do not replace a doctor but may be used for prevention or quick relief until medical help is available. To learn them, learners speak to parents, grandparents or elders to identify simple remedies common in Indian households, some of which, like herbal teas and turmeric milk, are recommended in AYUSH, while others like aloe vera gel are family traditions. Examples include turmeric milk for immunity, made by warming milk with half a teaspoon of turmeric and a little jaggery; tulasi-ginger tea, made by boiling water with crushed ginger and tulasi leaves and adding honey; and aloe vera gel for dry skin, applied gently after a patch test. These are not medical treatments, must be prepared under adult guidance, and learners only demonstrate and explain, not give remedies to camp visitors.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Home remedies are simple natural cures for small problems, like turmeric milk for immunity. They do not replace a doctor and must be made with an adult's help, and you only show them, not give them to visitors.
stepByStep: Learn from elders: Identify common household remedies from family.
Note the source: Recognise some are from AYUSH, others from tradition.
Prepare turmeric milk: Warm milk with turmeric and a little jaggery.
Make herbal tea: Boil ginger and tulasi leaves with honey.
Demonstrate safely: Only demonstrate under adult guidance, not give remedies.

studyContent.title: Common Occupational Hazards and Prevention
studyContent.intro: Identifying work-related health risks and simple preventive measures.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Different kinds of work put different strains on the body, as long hours, repeated movements, heat, noise, pollution or uncomfortable postures can slowly harm health; for example a rickshaw driver may develop back pain, a tailor eye strain, a cook heat and smoke exposure, and a computer user neck pain. Understanding these occupational hazards and practising simple preventive actions help people stay healthy. Examples include eye strain for computer users, prevented by the 20-20-20 rule, correct screen height and neck stretches; back pain from long sitting, prevented by straight posture, lumbar support and standing breaks; muscle injuries from lifting, prevented by bending the knees; breathing problems in polluted areas, prevented by masks and hydration; hearing strain in noise, prevented by ear protection; and heat stress, prevented by drinking water and resting in shade. Preventive measures include posture correction, stretching exercises, breathing routines and simple yogasanas done under adult supervision.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Different jobs cause different health problems, like back pain from sitting or eye strain from screens. Simple steps like good posture, stretches, breaks and yoga help workers stay healthy.
stepByStep: Identify the hazard: Spot risks like back pain, eye strain or heat stress.
Match to the job: Link hazards to occupations like drivers or tailors.
Apply prevention: Use posture correction and the 20-20-20 rule.
Do stretches and breathing: Practise stretches, breathing and yogasanas.
Work under supervision: Practise yoga safely under an adult's guidance.

keywords.meaning: A health risk caused by one's work.
keywords.example: Back pain is an occupational hazard for drivers.

keywords.meaning: Looking 20 m away for 20 seconds every 20 minutes.
keywords.example: The 20-20-20 rule reduces eye strain.

keywords.meaning: Adjusting body position to avoid strain.
keywords.example: Sitting straight is posture correction.

studyContent.title: Emergency Response and Health Apps
studyContent.intro: Knowing basic first-aid responses and exploring health support apps.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Basic first-aid skills like cleaning small wounds, cooling burns and preparing oral rehydration solution help learners respond calmly until a trained adult arrives, and they can prepare posters and demonstrate safe steps for common situations like fever, heat cramps, nosebleeds, small cuts or snakebites, listing symptoms, how to respond and what not to do. Important safety notes are that first aid is only the first response and does not replace medical care, an adult must always be called first, medicines or home remedies must not be given, and advanced procedures like CPR must only be done by trained adults. Learners also explore mobile apps and platforms that support health and well-being, such as e-Sanjeevani for free online doctor consultation, CoWIN or ABHA for vaccination and digital health ID, FIT India for fitness, mYoga for guided yoga, step counter apps, and MANODARPAN for students' mental well-being.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Basic first aid is the first help you give until an adult arrives, like cleaning a cut. Always call an adult first and never give medicine. Helpful apps like e-Sanjeevani and mYoga support health too.
stepByStep: Learn basic first aid: Know how to clean wounds and cool burns.
Prepare response posters: List symptoms, responses and what not to do.
Call an adult first: Always seek a trained adult before acting further.
Avoid medicines and CPR: Do not give medicines or attempt advanced procedures.
Explore health apps: Use apps like e-Sanjeevani, mYoga and MANODARPAN.

studyContent.title: Providing and Promoting the Health Service
studyContent.intro: Promoting the service and delivering it with a checklist and event plan.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Promoting the service informs people and encourages them to act on what they learn, through simple posters with clear information on date, time, place and services, short announcements during assemblies or community meetings, informing parents and neighbours through community walks, displaying notices at schools, anganwadis or shops, and using notice boards or social media with permission. Providing the service applies earlier Kaushal Mela learnings, involving planning, setting up the space, delivering the service and completing post-event tasks like documentation and cleaning. Before the service, an event checklist finalises the processes for the day and assigns responsibilities, such as health-parameter screening, demonstrations of first aid, yogasanas and home remedies, and meetings with a doctor and nurse, while a resource checklist ensures items like tables, chairs, dustbins, instruments, a first-aid kit, gloves and sanitiser are available in sufficient quantity.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Tell people about your health service with posters and announcements. On the day, follow a checklist so everything like tables, instruments and gloves is ready, set up the space, deliver the service and clean up.
stepByStep: Make promotional material: Create posters with date, time, place and services.
Announce widely: Use assemblies, community walks and notice boards.
Prepare an event checklist: Finalise the day's processes and assign roles.
Check resources: Ensure tables, instruments, gloves and sanitiser are ready.
Deliver and clean up: Set up, deliver the service and complete documentation.

studyContent.title: Introduction to Tourism
studyContent.intro: How tourism lets people explore India's diverse places and cultures.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: India is a country where the land, language, food and stories change every few kilometres, and each place offers something unique whether culture, monuments, places of worship, landscapes, food, sports, festivals or museums; while some places are developed tourist sites, others remain off the beaten path, and the tourism industry helps people visit places that enrich them. People travel to rest and rejuvenate or to learn from different cultures, and travel helps us understand people beyond our surroundings and carry messages of equality, diversity and compassion. Behind every pleasant journey are guides, drivers, artisans, photographers and hospitality staff, and Indian culture believes in Atithi Devo Bhava, that guests are equivalent to gods. Different travel experiences include homestays, tribal and cultural heritage tourism, cycling and walking trails, river, lake and forest tourism, and spiritual and wellness journeys, all guided by responsible tourism that leaves no waste and protects the habitat.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: India has amazing places to visit, from monuments to forests. Tourism helps people explore them and learn about other cultures. We treat guests like gods and practise responsible tourism that protects nature.
stepByStep: See India's diversity: Note how places, food and culture change every few kilometres.
Understand why people travel: Recognise rest, learning and cultural understanding.
Value those behind tourism: Appreciate guides, drivers, artisans and staff.
Welcome guests: Apply Atithi Devo Bhava, treating guests as gods.
Practise responsible tourism: Leave no waste and protect the habitat.

studyContent.title: Scoping and Process Chart for Tourism
studyContent.intro: Deciding the tourism service to offer and listing the tasks.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Scoping the tourism work involves deciding the type of service based on where the school is located, such as a visit to an old school, a festival celebration, a mandi, a famous food street or a religious site that interests visitors. Decisions cover the availability of materials and resources like maps, photographs, digital devices and stationery, and access to elders who can tell interesting stories; the usefulness and relevance of the work, such as whether it highlights local heritage or helps tourists navigate; and where the service will be delivered, within the school as an exhibition or video, or outside at nearby sites, considering accessibility, safety, distance and any permissions needed. The service should be small-scale and manageable. A process chart then lists tasks such as a preparatory visit to the site, gathering information, developing a route map, preparing promotional materials and refreshments, promoting the service, delivering it, and feedback and reflection.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Decide what tourism service you can offer, like a heritage walk near your school, and check you have maps, devices and people who know stories. Then list the tasks, from visiting the site to guiding tourists and getting feedback.
stepByStep: Choose the service: Pick a walk, festival visit or food-street tour nearby.
Check resources: Confirm maps, devices, stationery and storytellers.
Judge usefulness: Decide if it highlights heritage or aids navigation.
Decide the location: Plan within or near the school, checking permissions.
Make the process chart: List tasks from site visit to feedback.

studyContent.title: Site Visit and Selecting Materials and Tools
studyContent.intro: Visiting a tourism site, learning from experts and gathering materials.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Before beginning the work, learners visit a tourism-related site and interact with an expert such as a tourist guide, travel agent, hotel or homestay staff, museum attendant or local artisan, in the presence of a teacher, to learn the tools and materials used, key processes, scheduling, safety and confidentiality considerations, quality and visitor satisfaction, and use of technology like online booking or QR codes. After the visit, the process chart is finalised. Materials for tourism include stationery for posters and signages (reused and recycled), dustbins or disposable bags for waste (placed away from walking paths), water bottles for hydration (clean and reusable), and a safety kit with basic first-aid items (checking expiry dates). Tools include a tablet or smartphone for digital mapping, photographs and information (used responsibly), a map or GPS to guide tourists and plan routes (verified in advance), and a speaker to address a group (kept at low volume).
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Visit a tourism site and ask an expert how they work and stay safe. Then gather your materials, like stationery, dustbins, water and a first-aid kit, and tools like a tablet, a map and a speaker.
stepByStep: Visit the site: Go with a teacher to a tourism-related site.
Learn from the expert: Ask about tools, processes, safety and technology.
Finalise the chart: Update the process chart after the visit.
Gather materials: Collect stationery, dustbins, water and a safety kit.
Select tools: Use a tablet, map or GPS and a speaker responsibly.

topicTitle: Bill of Materials and Consent Form
sourceLabel: Based on NCERT Kaushal Vikas (Grade 9) + AI-generated teacher-style explanation

studyContent.title: Bill of Materials and Consent Form
studyContent.intro: Estimating tourism service costs and preparing a consent form.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Every service has a cost, some small and visible like chart paper for a map or pens, and others less visible like the time spent in preparation; unpredictable situations such as delays, cancellations and weather changes also increase costs, and professionals must account for the limited tourist season and higher costs in remote areas. The Bill of Materials covers both materials bought and the effort to provide the service, listing items like markers, waste bags, itinerary sheets, chart papers, a borrowed first-aid kit, gloves and a borrowed smartphone and speaker, plus labour for cleaning and waste disposal, guiding tourists and preparation. A consent form, as learnt earlier, clearly communicates what the provider will offer, what the tourist can expect and each person's responsibility; it is not a legal document but builds trust, and it should share what is provided, share safety expectations, clarify charges and refunds, and take consent for photographs.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: List the costs of your tourism service, including markers, waste bags and the time you spend. A consent form then tells tourists what you offer, the safety rules and charges, and asks permission for photos.
stepByStep: List the costs: Note visible costs like markers and hidden ones like time.
Account for uncertainty: Allow for delays, weather and remote-area costs.
Make the Bill of Materials: List materials and labour for the service.
Prepare the consent form: Communicate what is provided and expected.
Cover charges and photos: Clarify charges, refunds and photo consent.

studyContent.title: Planning for Safety and Accessibility
studyContent.intro: Keeping tourists safe and accommodating persons with disabilities.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: One of the most important responsibilities of a tourism service provider is to keep tourists safe, which also means taking care of one's own safety and protecting the place or property where the service is delivered; simple safety points apply to most tourism activities and more can be added based on location. An important consideration in planning is accessibility, so that persons with disabilities are accommodated by identifying their needs and how to meet them; for example, identifying locations of accessible toilets, checking whether restaurants have ramps, and carefully going over a guided-tour route to find any stretches that may be hard to negotiate while identifying easier alternative paths. Learners decide what arrangements will help persons with disabilities fully participate and can explore apps that help accommodate special needs, asking an expert for guidance.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Safety is the most important duty in tourism, for the tourists, yourself and the place. You must also plan for accessibility, making sure people with disabilities can reach toilets, ramps and easier paths.
stepByStep: Prioritise safety: Keep tourists, yourself and the property safe.
Apply safety points: Use general safety points and add location-specific ones.
Consider accessibility: Identify the needs of persons with disabilities.
Check the route: Find accessible toilets, ramps and easier paths.
Use helpful apps: Explore apps that accommodate special needs.

keywords.example: Ramps improve accessibility.

keywords.example: Safety planning protects tourists.

keywords.example: An alternative path avoids steep stretches.

studyContent.title: Preparing a Tourism Map
studyContent.intro: Making a clear map showing attractions, facilities and routes.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: A map is one of the most important tools in tourism, helping tourists understand a place, locate landmarks and plan their movement, and in tourism it is used not only for navigation but to highlight attractions, facilities, and cultural or natural features. Unlike detailed geographical maps, tourism maps focus on places of interest such as heritage sites, parks, temples, markets and museums; useful facilities such as washrooms, drinking water, parking, bus stops and eateries; and routes and directions that help tourists move safely and comfortably. Tourism maps are drawn with clear symbols, colours, labels and short descriptions so people from different backgrounds can easily understand them. In one example, students created a map detailing the route from their school to a heritage site and back, indicating spots for refreshment, clean water and toilets along the way, and learners can also mark places on a downloaded map or create a blog around it.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A tourism map shows tourists where the interesting places, toilets, water and routes are. It uses clear symbols and colours so anyone can understand it and find their way comfortably.
stepByStep: Mark places of interest: Show heritage sites, temples, markets and museums.
Show facilities: Indicate washrooms, water, parking and eateries.
Draw the routes: Add directions for safe, comfortable movement.
Use clear symbols: Apply symbols, colours, labels and short descriptions.
Make it accessible: Mark facilities so everyone can understand and use it.

keywords.example: A tourism map marks the heritage walk.

keywords.example: A temple is a landmark on the map.

keywords.example: Drinking water is a facility on the map.

studyContent.title: Making an Information Brochure
studyContent.intro: Collecting and presenting interesting local information for tourists.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: The history or stories connected to a place, and any specialty of culture, language or cuisine, are interesting to tourists. Recalling the AI Assistant project, categories of interest include food, flora and fauna, dance and music, places of interest, and common terms in the local language. In addition, if there are museums or theatres, then timings, weekly holidays, entrance fees and parking will be important for tourists. Learners collect information related to their service from books, the internet, older people in the community or local guides, and present it in a short, clear handout or brochure. This brochure becomes a key communication tool that helps tourists understand and appreciate the place they are visiting while making the service more useful and enriching.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A brochure is a short handout that shares interesting facts about a place, like its history, food, culture and timings. You gather this from books, the internet and local elders to make the tour more enjoyable.
stepByStep: Identify the categories: Cover food, culture, language and places of interest.
Add practical details: Include timings, holidays, fees and parking.
Gather information: Collect from books, the internet and local elders.
Keep it clear: Present it in a short, clear handout.
Make it useful: Help tourists understand and appreciate the place.

studyContent.title: Promoting the Tourism Service
studyContent.intro: Encouraging visitors to take part through promotional materials.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Promoting the tourism service is important not only to inform people but also to encourage them to take part in what is offered, and it follows the same principles used for other services. Learners can make simple promotional materials such as posters and signages with clear information, create maps and route guides, and use digital methods like a blog around the map or reels posted in a closed group for tourists who prefer digital versions. The promotional material should clearly convey the attractions, the meeting point, the route and the facilities available, making the service appealing and easy to join. Effective promotion increases participation while ensuring that the information shared is accurate, respectful and helpful, encouraging tourists to explore the locality and appreciate its heritage and culture.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: To get tourists to join, you promote your service with posters, maps and even online reels. The promotion should clearly show what is special, where to meet and what facilities are available, so people want to come.
stepByStep: Decide the message: Highlight the attractions and the meeting point.
Make posters: Create clear posters and signages with key details.
Share the route: Include the map and route in the promotion.
Use digital methods: Post a blog or reels in a closed tourist group.
Keep it accurate: Ensure information is correct, respectful and helpful.

studyContent.title: Delivering the Service and Collecting Feedback
studyContent.intro: Providing the tourism service responsibly and reflecting on feedback.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: After all preparation, the tourism service is delivered, applying the quality criteria of input, process and output stages learnt in Chapter 9. During delivery the team welcomes tourists, guides them safely along the planned route using the map and brochure, manages waste with labelled dustbins, ensures hydration and accessibility, and maintains respectful, clear communication while keeping the place clean and undisturbed. Quality is monitored during the service by watching for confusion or discomfort and adjusting as needed, and special care is taken for elders, children and persons with disabilities. After the service, feedback is collected on visitor satisfaction and comfort, the team reflects on what worked well and what challenges arose, and post-service tasks like cleaning up, disposing waste responsibly and packing materials are completed. This reflection helps improve the service the next time.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: On the day, welcome the tourists and guide them safely along the route, keeping the place clean and being polite. Afterwards, ask for feedback, clean up, and think about what went well so you can do better next time.
stepByStep: Welcome the tourists: Greet and guide them along the planned route.
Manage the tour: Handle waste, hydration, accessibility and communication.
Monitor quality: Watch for confusion and support elders and children.
Collect feedback: Ask about visitor satisfaction and comfort.
Clean up and reflect: Dispose waste, pack materials and reflect to improve.

keywords.example: Guiding the walk is service delivery.

keywords.meaning: Comments from users about the service.
keywords.example: Feedback shows visitor satisfaction.

keywords.meaning: Thinking back to improve future service.

studyContent.title: Additional Vocations in Human Services
studyContent.intro: An overview of five further service vocations learners can choose.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Chapter 12 contains guidelines for five additional vocations in human services: hospitality, event management, data-based services, interior design and public information service. These build on the common concepts and processes from Chapter 9 while introducing concepts specific to each. The vocations broadly follow a similar sequence of identifying a need or opportunity, planning the service and roles, preparing the service environment, delivering the service, monitoring quality during the service, collecting feedback and recording learnings, and completing post-service activities. For each vocation the chapter lists key materials, quality parameters and safety parameters. Learners may choose any of these vocations or something entirely different related to human services, always consulting a teacher or expert and working in groups, since a big part of service is working together with others, for others.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Besides personal services, healthcare and tourism, you can choose other service work like hospitality, event management, data services, interior design or public information. Each follows similar steps of planning, delivering and reviewing a service.
stepByStep: List the options: Note hospitality, event management, data services, interior design and public information.
Recall the sequence: See the shared steps of plan, deliver and review.
Check key materials: Identify the special materials each vocation needs.
Mind quality and safety: Follow quality and safety parameters for each.
Choose and consult: Select a vocation in a group with expert guidance.

studyContent.title: Hospitality
studyContent.intro: Welcoming and caring for visitors so they feel comfortable and respected.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Work in hospitality includes welcoming, guiding and taking care of visitors so they feel comfortable, respected and safe, and good hospitality depends on polite communication, clean and well-organised spaces, clear guidance and care for people with different needs such as children, elders and persons with disabilities. Key concepts include guest experience (how comfortable and supported a guest feels) and etiquette (polite behaviour showing respect through greeting, tone, posture and listening). Materials include signage and placards, seating arrangements, cleanliness items, team attire with name tags, recording materials, a safety kit and serving items. The process identifies a real situation needing hospitality like a school event, plans roles such as greeters, guides and servers, prepares the environment by marking entry, exit and queue paths, practises greeting and handling situations, delivers by welcoming and serving safely, monitors quality watching for crowding, collects feedback and completes post-service cleanup.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Hospitality means making visitors feel welcome, comfortable and respected through polite manners, clean spaces and clear guidance. You greet guests, guide them, serve them safely and care for their different needs.
stepByStep: Identify the situation: Choose an event needing hospitality service.
Plan the roles: Assign greeters, guides, servers and cleanliness teams.
Prepare the environment: Mark entry, exit, seating and queue paths.
Practise and deliver: Practise greeting, then welcome and serve guests safely.
Monitor and review: Watch for crowding, collect feedback and clean up.

keywords.example: A warm welcome improves guest experience.

keywords.meaning: Polite behaviour showing respect.
keywords.example: Greeting with a smile is etiquette.

studyContent.title: Event Management
studyContent.intro: Planning and managing events that bring people together for a purpose.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Event management is the work of planning, organising and managing events that bring people together for a purpose, involving understanding the event's purpose, planning the programme flow, coordinating people and materials, managing guests, and responding calmly to unexpected situations. Key concepts include ambience (the overall atmosphere created through decor, lighting, music and arrangement), programme flow (the sequence in which activities take place) and logistics (planning and managing materials, equipment, people and time). Materials include stationery for schedules and banners, a sound system, tables and chairs, hospitality items and a first-aid kit. The process identifies a school or community event and its purpose and scale, plans the service and roles with a process chart, prepares by deciding theme, stage, seating, decor and signage and conducting a rehearsal, delivers by managing setup and programme flow, monitors quality, collects feedback and completes post-service cleanup.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Event management is planning events like school functions. You decide the purpose, the order of activities and the atmosphere, arrange everything, rehearse it, run the event smoothly and handle surprises calmly.
stepByStep: Identify the event: Select an event and understand its purpose and scale.
Plan roles and chart: Make a process chart for flow, logistics and safety.
Prepare the setup: Decide theme, stage, seating, decor and rehearse.
Deliver the event: Manage setup, welcome guests and run the programme flow.
Monitor and review: Watch for crowding, collect feedback and clean up.

keywords.example: Lighting sets the event's ambience.

keywords.example: The programme flow lists each performance.

studyContent.title: Data-based Services
studyContent.intro: Collecting, organising and analysing information to support decisions.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Data-based services involve collecting, organising, analysing and using information to support decision-making for people and communities, and when data is collected carefully and used responsibly it helps schools, families, communities and governments understand problems, identify patterns and plan improvements, supporting services in education, health, governance, environment, welfare and local development. Key concepts include data analysis (studying data to identify patterns and trends), data visualisation (showing data using charts, graphs or tables), survey (collecting information from people using questions) and insight (meaningful understanding drawn from data that helps decision-making). Materials include a computer or tablet, supervised smartphone, survey forms, a notebook and consent forms. The process identifies a real problem like attendance or water use, prepares survey questions, collects information with consent and privacy, analyses and visualises it in tables and charts, interprets what it shows, monitors ethics and accuracy, and presents the findings.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Data-based services collect and study information to help make decisions. You ask people questions in a survey, organise the answers into charts, and find useful patterns, always with consent and care for privacy.
stepByStep: Identify the problem: Choose a real issue like attendance or water use.
Prepare the survey: Make simple, relevant, unbiased questions.
Collect with consent: Gather data respecting privacy and permission.
Analyse and visualise: Sort responses and make tables and charts.
Interpret and present: Find insights and present the findings.

keywords.meaning: Collecting information from people using questions.
keywords.example: A survey gathers data on attendance.

keywords.meaning: Showing data using charts, graphs or tables.
keywords.example: A bar chart is data visualisation.

keywords.meaning: Meaningful understanding drawn from data.
keywords.example: An insight shows peak water-use times.

studyContent.title: Interior Design
studyContent.intro: Planning indoor spaces to be comfortable, functional, safe and pleasing.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Interior design involves planning and improving indoor spaces so they are comfortable, functional, safe and visually pleasing, by understanding how people use a space and making thoughtful changes in layout, colour, lighting, furniture and decor to improve everyday use. Key concepts include layout (the arrangement of furniture and elements in a space), theme (the overall style or idea used in designing a space) and sustainability (using materials and methods that reduce waste and protect the environment). Materials include a measuring tape, ruler or set square, stationery for sketches and mood boards, cardboard or recycled boxes for models, and paint swatches or fabric samples. The process identifies an indoor space like a classroom or study corner that needs improvement and speaks to users, plans by measuring and drawing a floor plan and deciding furniture, colours and lighting, prepares drawings or 3D models within a budget, delivers by arranging the space, monitors quality, collects feedback and reflects.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Interior design means improving an indoor space, like a classroom, to be comfortable and nice to look at. You measure it, plan the layout and colours, make a model, then arrange the furniture and decor.
stepByStep: Identify the space: Choose an indoor space that needs improvement.
Understand the users: Speak to users about comfort, storage and lighting.
Plan the layout: Measure the space and draw a floor plan with furniture.
Make a model: Build a model from cardboard within a budget.
Deliver and review: Arrange the space, collect feedback and reflect.

studyContent.title: Public Information Service
studyContent.intro: Helping citizens access information, fill forms and track requests.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Public information service helps citizens who face difficulties because they do not know how to get information, fill forms or track requests, especially when services are online, and it plays an important role in ensuring transparency, inclusion and timely access to benefits related to education, health, housing, utilities and social welfare. These services are provided through Common Service Centres, e-Seva Kendras, panchayat or municipal offices, help desks and helplines. Key concepts include e-governance (use of digital technology to provide government services), transparency (making government processes open and easy to understand) and grievance (a complaint raised to request action). Materials include a computer or tablet, supervised smartphone, paper forms, a notebook and a consent form. The process identifies common services like scholarship forms or certificate updates, learns the purpose, documents and process, notes information with consent, delivers by visiting verified government websites and submitting applications, follows up using reference numbers, and collects feedback.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Public information service helps people who do not know how to get information or fill government forms online. You learn the correct process, help them apply on official sites, and track their request to completion.
stepByStep: Identify the service: Choose a common need like a scholarship or certificate.
Learn the process: Understand the documents, eligibility and official process.
Note information: Record basic details with the person's consent.
Deliver the help: Apply on verified government websites and submit.
Follow up: Use reference numbers to track and explain next steps.

keywords.example: Transparency lets citizens track requests.

keywords.example: A grievance reports a service problem.

topicTitle: Quality and Safety Parameters in Service Vocations
sourceLabel: Based on NCERT Kaushal Vikas (Grade 9) + AI-generated teacher-style explanation

studyContent.title: Quality and Safety Parameters in Service Vocations
studyContent.intro: The common quality and safety standards across the service vocations.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Each additional service vocation lists distinct quality and safety parameters. Quality parameters generally focus on a clear purpose matching the need, polite and respectful treatment of people, clarity of communication, ethical handling of information used only for its stated purpose, inclusion of all users, and effective outcomes; for hospitality this means polite welcome and comfort, while for data-based services it means reliable collection and useful insight. Safety parameters across services stress data privacy by not sharing personal details, documents or OTPs, taking consent before collecting information, using only verified and trusted sources, maintaining digital hygiene by logging out and avoiding unknown links, ensuring safe crowd movement and clear exits, keeping first-aid ready, and disposing of waste responsibly. Following these parameters protects both the quality of the service and the safety, privacy and trust of the people receiving it.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Every service has rules for good quality and safety. Quality means a clear purpose, polite treatment, clear communication and ethical use of information. Safety means protecting people's data, taking consent and keeping exits clear.
stepByStep: Set a clear purpose: Match the service to the real need.
Treat people well: Communicate politely, clearly and inclusively.
Handle data ethically: Use information only for its stated purpose.
Protect privacy: Never share personal details, documents or OTPs.
Keep things safe: Maintain digital hygiene, clear exits and first aid.

keywords.example: Clear communication is a quality parameter.

keywords.meaning: Keeping personal information protected.
keywords.example: Data privacy means not sharing OTPs.

keywords.example: Logging out maintains digital hygiene.

subject: Skill Education
sourceLabel: Based on NCERT Kaushal Vikas (Grade 9) + AI-generated teacher-style explanation

studyContent.title: Common Process Across Service Vocations
studyContent.intro: The shared sequence used to plan and deliver any human service.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Although each service vocation differs, they all follow a common broad sequence that learners can apply to any chosen work. The first step is identifying the need or opportunity, choosing a real situation where the service is required. The second is planning the service and roles, deciding responsibilities and requirements. The third is preparation, setting up the service environment, materials and rehearsals as relevant. The fourth is delivery, providing the service while welcoming and supporting people. The fifth is monitoring quality during the service, watching for confusion, crowding or discomfort and adjusting as needed. The sixth is collecting feedback and recording learnings, noting what worked, what challenges arose and what to improve. The final step is post-service activities, cleaning up, disposing waste responsibly and reflecting on improvements. This shared sequence helps learners deliver any human service systematically and with care.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Every service follows the same basic steps: find a real need, plan the roles, prepare everything, deliver the service, watch the quality, collect feedback, and clean up while reflecting on how to improve.
stepByStep: Identify the need: Choose a real situation needing the service.
Plan roles: Decide responsibilities and requirements.
Prepare the service: Set up the environment, materials and rehearsals.
Deliver the service: Provide it while welcoming and supporting people.
Monitor and review: Watch quality, collect feedback and clean up.

keywords.meaning: The ordered steps to deliver a service.
keywords.example: The sequence runs from need to review.

keywords.meaning: Watching the service to keep it good.
keywords.example: Monitoring spots crowding during an event.

keywords.example: Disposing waste is a post-service activity.
